coredata.dc:title: Photoelectrochemical activity of graphene quantum dots/hierarchical porous TiO2 photoanode
coredata.dc:creator: Azimirad, R.
Safa, S.
Ebrahimi, M.
Yousefzadeh, S.
Moshfegh, A.Z.
coredata.dc:description: In this study, initially graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by an electrochemical technique and hierarchical porous TiO2 were made by a sol – gel method. Subsequently, GQDs/hierarchical porous TiO2 nanocomposites were prepared by two different methods for the purpose of comparison: spin coating (SC) and electrophoretic (EP) deposition. The GQDs/hierarchical porous TiO2 nanocomposites were characterized by various analytical methods including field emission scanning electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, high resolution transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infra-red spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy and ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy. Under visible light irradiation, photoanodes made of the hierarchical porous TiO2, EP- GQDs/hierarchical porous TiO2 and SC- GQDs/hierarchical porous TiO2 nanocomposite produced the photocurrent densities of about 1.86, 3.29 and 6.35 A/m2, respectively. The results showed that the nanocomposites prepared by the SC technique exhibit a higher photoelectrochemical (PEC) activity as compared to both the EP deposited composite and the pure hierarchical porous TiO2. Finally, the photoenhancement was described based on a charge transfer mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hierarchical porous
Graphene quantum dots
Photocatalyst
TiO2

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic view of the fabrication processes of (a) GQDs, (b) TiO2 porous, (c) SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous and (d) EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous.
FESEM images of (a) the pure TiO2 porous, (b) EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite and (c) SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite. The insets show the high-magnification images.

AFM images of the surface of (a) the TiO2 porous film and (b) the GQDs on the mica surface, the height profile related to the dotted line is shown in the inset. (c) TEM image of the GQDs with their size distribution. The HRTEM image of a GQD is shown in the inset.

(a) UV–visible absorption spectrum (dotted line) and PL emission spectra of the GQDs aqueous solution. Inset shows the photograph of the aqueous solution excited by a UV lamp (355 nm). (b) UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra of the GQDs/TiO2 porous (prepared by spin coating and electrophoretic deposition methods) as compared with the pure TiO2 porous.

Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) plots for the (a) TiO2 porous, (b) the EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite and (c) the SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite photoanodes in dark and (d) the TiO2 porous, (e) the EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite and (f) the SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite photoanodes under visible irradiation (1000 W/m2).

Photocurrent density versus time at 0.5 V vs Ag/AgCl reference electrode for (a) TiO2 porous, (b) EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite and (c) SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite photoanodes under visible photoirradiation (1000 W/m2).
Mott–Schottky plots of (a) TiO2 porous, (b) EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous and (c) SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous.
The proposed mechanism for the photoelectrochemical water splitting process by (a) SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite and (b) EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite.
A brief summary on the photoresponse of different porous nanostructures.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Bias voltage was measured vs. Ag/AgCl electrode.

head.ce:title: Photoelectrochemical activity of graphene quantum dots/hierarchical porous TiO2 photoanode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Malek–Ashtar University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Sharif University of Technology, P.O. Box 11155-9161, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Sahand University of Technology, P.O. Box 51335-1996, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Sharif University of Technology, P.O.Box 14588-89694, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, initially graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by an electrochemical technique and hierarchical porous TiO2 were made by a sol – gel method. Subsequently, GQDs/hierarchical porous TiO2 nanocomposites were prepared by two different methods for the purpose of comparison: spin coating (SC) and electrophoretic (EP) deposition. The GQDs/hierarchical porous TiO2 nanocomposites were characterized by various analytical methods including field emission scanning electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, high resolution transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infra-red spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy and ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy. Under visible light irradiation, photoanodes made of the hierarchical porous TiO2, EP- GQDs/hierarchical porous TiO2 and SC- GQDs/hierarchical porous TiO2 nanocomposite produced the photocurrent densities of about 1.86, 3.29 and 6.35 A/m2, respectively. The results showed that the nanocomposites prepared by the SC technique exhibit a higher photoelectrochemical (PEC) activity as compared to both the EP deposited composite and the pure hierarchical porous TiO2. Finally, the photoenhancement was described based on a charge transfer mechanism.

ce:section.ce:para: Fossil fuels are non-renewable resources which constitute about 80% of all energy supplies [1]. These types of fuels create air pollution, greenhouse effect and climate change leading to severe environmental concerns. Thus, one of the main challenges for the human beings is to find an alternative resource with a clean and renewable source of energy. Solar energy is one of the most abundant and easily accessible energy resource among all renewable sources. In this regard, capturing sunlight and its subsequent conversion into chemical energy have attracted considerable attention [2]. In this approach, photoelectrochemical cells (PECs) are among the most attractive systems for this conversion leading to hydrogen production. A PEC water splitting reaction can occur during irradiation of a semiconductor material as a photoanode. This leads to the photo assisted generation of electron and hole pairs which can interact with surface species, resulting in the production of hydrogen at the cathode and oxygen at the anode without producing greenhouse gases [3].

Investigation on appropriate semiconductor photocatalysts that can be harvest a wide range of sunlight wavelengths from ultra violet (UV) to near infrared (NIR) for an efficient production of clean energy is the subject of many published papers [4–8].

Among photocatalysts, TiO2 is one of the most promising catalysts because of its relatively low cost, superior photocatalytic performance, long-term stability, and non-toxicity [9]. Unfortunately, its wide band gap (3.2 eV) limits its widespread applications as it can become active under UV light only, which accounts for less than 4% of the total incoming solar radiation [10–13]. To overcome this challenge, many strategies have been used such as doping with metals and nonmetal elements [14–16], coupling with narrow band gap semiconductors [17–19], loading with noble metals [20–22] and controlling the morphology [23,24].

Structure and morphology of TiO2 play main roles in its PEC performance. Recently, synthesis and application of TiO2 films with a porous structure as 3D nanostructures with a high specific surface area, efficient charge separation and transfer, easy and high ability to adsorb reactive element (such as dyes or quantum dots (QDs)) and enhanced light harvesting due to multiscattering of incident light have become a hot research topic in material science [25,26]. Among all porous type structures, hierarchical porous materials with at least bimodal pore size distribution have attracted attentions in solar conversion application [27,28]. In a hierarchical porous TiO2, mesopores create a large surface area for reaction at the interface and macropores transport reactants or redox couples to the interface and provide micro-channels [29,30]. There are various methods for preparation of the hierarchical porous TiO2 such as phase separation, mesoporous particles assembly, template-assisted method etc. [31]. The phase separation method is easy and template-free approach that hierarchical porous films and bulk TiO2 are produced by using long-chained molecules in combination with sol–gel method [32]. Based on this synthesis method, Guli et al. fabricated hierarchical porous TiO2 photoanode for dye sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) by photoinduced polymerization and a spinodal phase separation method [33]. Their results showed that a macroporous continuous skeleton enhances the electron and a porous structure improves light absorption and dye adsorption. To the best of our knowledge, hierarchical porous TiO2 films with prominent physical and chemical properties and prepared by a phase separation method have not been utilized as a photoanode in PEC cells. On the other hand, the PEC performance of these structures can be modified by different carbon nanostructures. Various carbon nanostructures including carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [34], carbon nanofibers (CNFs) [35], graphene [36] and graphene oxide [37] have been widely studied due to their unique properties such as a low cost [38], high surface area [39] and excellent electronic properties [40] which are interesting in energy storage applications [41], transparent conductive films [42] and environmental issues [43]. Recently, carbon-based quantum dots (C-QDs) as a new class of carbon nanostructures has attracted a widespread attention in many applications, especially in energy applications [44]. Recently, C-QDs have been extensively reviewed for photocatalytic applications [45]. Among C-QDs, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are mono or few-layer graphene sheets with lateral dimensions of less than 100 nm [46]. The GQDs have unique properties, such as their excellent up-conversion and size dependent photoluminescence, robust chemical inertness, high aqueous solubility and low cytotoxicity [47], and they are widely utilized in various branches of sciences including biology [48], sensors [49] and energy conversion [50]. Very recently, GQDs have also been studied for photocatalytic applications [51–54]. The strong chemical interaction between the GQDs and semiconductors make their composites more stable having a relatively better charge transfer, leading to a higher photocatalytic activity [55]. Additionally, to the best of our knowledge, there are no reports on heterojunction formation between GQDs and porous TiO2 nanostructures for the PEC water splitting application. Recent photoelectrochemical studies on porous nanostructures are summarized in Table 1 .

Quantum dots (QDs) of materials such as CdS, CdSe and PbS have attracted attention as photosensitizers to sensitize wide band gap semiconductors like TiO2 for H2 production in the visible light region. However, the current QDs are limited due to their toxicity resulting from the presence of heavy metals. Among various QDs, GQDs were chosen in this study, because they are a non-toxic photosensitizer and have a low cost and easy fabrication process. In this work, the hierarchical porous TiO2 films were synthesized by facile sol–gel and phase separation methods. Then, the GQDs synthesized with an electrochemical process were deposited on the hierarchical porous TiO2 (GQDs/TiO2 porous) by two different techniques for the purpose of comparison: spin coating and electrophoretic deposition. The GQDs/TiO2 porous films were utilized for photocatalytic H2 production from an aqueous solution under visible light irradiation as compared with the pure porous TiO2. Furthermore, the mechanism of the enhance water splitting and H2 production is proposed and schematically discussed.

Deionized water purified with Milli-Q System (Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA) was used during all of the preparation procedures. All other materials were commercially provided and used without any further purification. All chemicals in research grade were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Company.

The GQDs growth process was used based on Markovic et al. [64]. Typically, the electrolyte for electrochemical process was prepared by mixing ethanol/H2O (volume ratio 20:1) with 370 mg of NaOH. Two graphite rods (diameter of about 2 mm and separation distance of 2 cm) were inserted into the electrolyte as the anode and cathode. A static DC potential of 25 V was applied on the two electrodes using a direct current (DC) power supply. After 40 min interaction, a dark-yellow solution appeared gradually in the container. The resultant solution was centrifuged at 20000 rpm for 30 min to remove the precipitated graphite oxide and graphite particles. Finally, the GQDs solution was diluted by DI water.

Hierarchical porous TiO2 films (named as TiO2 porous) have been deposited on titanium foil by a sol–gel method as reported by others [32,65]. In a typical process, 1 mL of tetrabutyl orthotitanate (TBT, Ti(OC4H9)4) was dissolved in 4 mL ethanol (C2H5OH). The solution stirred for 30 min and then 1 mL of diethanol amine (DEA, NH(CH2CH2OH)2) was added to the solution as a stabilizer and the solution was stirred for 1.5 h in order to complete the ligand exchange reactions. Then, the hydrolyzing solution containing 500 μL of ethanol and 500 μL of water was dripped to the former solution and stirred for 1 h. Finally, 25 mg of polyethylene glycol (average molecular weight 4000, Merck) was also added to the solution. The resulting sol was stirred for another 1 h and maintained for 2 h in order to complete the hydrolysis reactions [32]. TiO2 porous films were deposited on the titanium foil substrates by dip coating method with a deposition speed of 60 mm/min. Then, the films were dried at 300 °C for 10 min. The process of dip coating and drying was repeated 5 times and finally the films were calcined at 550 °C for 1 h in air. The temperature was elevated slowly with a rate of 2 °Cmin−1.

GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposites were fabricated by two different methods for the purpose of comparison: spin coating and electrophoretic deposition. In the spin coating process, 50 μL of GQD solution was deposited on the prepared TiO2 porous as substrate with a spining speed of 1000 rpm for 30 s (SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite). In the second method, the electrophoretic deposition was performed in a two-electrode cell using the functionalized GQD aqueous solution with NaOH as an electrolyte, the deposited TiO2 porous as a anode and graphite electrode as a cathode. For deposition of the GQDs on the TiO2 porous films, a 5 V potential was applied to the electrodes for 10 s (EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite). These two types of samples were prepared in order to investigate and compare their PEC activity values as compared with the pure TiO2 porous without any added GQDs. Fig. 1 depicts the overall fabrication processes of the GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposites prepared with two different deposition approaches.

Surface morphologies of the porous TiO2 and GQDs were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy, FESEM, (ZEISS, SIGMA VP) and transmission electron microscopy, TEM, (Philips CM30 TEM). They were also studied by atomic force microscopy (AFM) using a commercial microscope (Veeco Autoprobe CP-research) in air with a Si tip with a 10 nm radius of curvature in the contact mode. Optical absorption spectra of the samples were investigated by UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) from 200 to 1000 nm with a resolution of 1 nm. Phototoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy (Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer) was used to study recombination processes of electrons and holes. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was recorded on a Bruker (VERTEX 70) spectrophotometer to investigate chemical bonding and it scanned from 4000 to 800 cm−1.

Photo-electrochemical (PEC) measurements were studied with a voltage controlled potentiostat system (AutolabPGSTAT302). A platinum wire was used as the counter electrode, Ag/AgCl was used as the reference electrode and the synthesized samples with an equal surface area for all measurements, were used as the working electrode and a 0.1 M Na2S aqueous solution was prepared as the electrolyte. Irradiation source was a Xenon short arc lamp (OSRAM, HBM/OFR) with a natural daylight continuous spectrum. Glassy IR filter sheet was placed in front of the lamp to avoid transmission of longer wavelengths. In addition, the lamp intensity was adjusted at 1000 W/m2 in the visible region. All measurements were performed in air and at room temperature (∼25 °C) using a three electrodes PEC cell. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) method was utilized to measure generated photocurrent densities in a potential range from −0.6–1 V versus the Ag/AgCl reference electrode. Chronoamperometry was used to measure photocurrent densities as a function of reaction time under a constant voltage.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement was performed in an electrochemical station, which used Pt wire as the working electrode, graphite plate as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as the reference. The CV profile was recorded in a 0.1 M KCl buffer in the presence of the sample suspension (1 mg/mL).

In this section, morphological and optical properties of the samples will be determined using different analytical techniques. To determine surface morphologies of the prepared samples, FESEM was employed. Fig. 2 (a) shows the FESEM image of the pure TiO2 porous film after synthesis. It is observed that the TiO2 film possesses porous layered structures which are uniformly distributed on the Ti substrate. The TiO2 porous film contains pores with an average diameter of ∼650 ± 340 nm. Fig. 2(b) and (c) show the FESEM images of the EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous and SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous, respectively. In the EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous sample, TiO2 surface is saturated with layers of graphite containing closed pores on the surface. But, in the SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous sample, GQDs are distributed on the surface with a size range less than 100 nm without closed pores on the surface. Fig. 3 (a) depicts the 2D-AFM image of the TiO2 porous film prepared by the sol gel method. It illustrates that the pores are formed with a circular structure on the surface. Based on the AFM analysis, pores diameter was measured ranging from 300 to 800 nm (in a good agreement with the SEM results) and surface roughness was determined about 22.30 nm. Moreover, Fig. 3(b) presents the 2D-AFM image of the GQDs solution drop casted on the mica surface. Fig. 3(b) inset shows a typical topographic height profile of the GQDs ranging from 0.8 to 2.0 nm. According to this measurement, the GQDs consist of 2–5 graphene layers. TEM image analysis provides further insights about the morphology of the synthesized GQDs. Fig. 3(c) shows that GQDs are relatively uniform in size ranging from 12 to 22 nm. The crystalline nature of the GQDs was then examined by High Resolution TEM (HR-TEM) measurements, as shown in the inset of Fig. 3(c). It clearly shows that the lattice spacing is around 0.32 nm, which corresponds to the (002) crystal phase of graphite [66,67].

Crystalline structure of the samples was also characterized by their XRD patterns. As shown in Fig. S1, a similar diffraction spectrum was obtained for the TiO2 porous and the SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous films. The XRD result shows that the presence of GQDs in the sample did not change any peak position in the diffraction pattern of the TiO2 porous film which is attributed to the low concentration of GQDs in the sample [68]. It was found that the structure of the sample is mainly comprised of the anatase phase (JCPDS 21-1272).

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the as synthesized GQDs is shown in Fig. S2. The broad absorption band at ∼3428 cm−1 corresponds to the OH stretching vibrations frequency attributed to vibrations of surface adsorbed water. The peaks at ∼3000, 1660, 1410 and 1050 cm−1 implied the existence of CH, CC, COH and CO groups, respectively [69,70]. Fig. S3 shows the FT-IR spectra of the pure TiO2 porous, SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous and EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous. The pure TiO2 exhibits a band at approximately 800 cm−1due to TiO stretching vibration [71]. The peak at 1420 cm−1 appeared in the SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous and the EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous composites are attributed to the COH vibrations in the GQDs.

UV–visible spectrum (dotted line) and photoluminescence spectra of GQDs were recorded and their results are shown in Fig. 4 (a). The UV–visible spectrum of GQDs, displays a strong optical absorption in the UV region with a tail extending into the visible region. The absorption peak around 240 nm corresponds to the π→ π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [72] and another absorption peak as a shoulder centered between 330 and 380 nm corresponds to the n→ π* transition of CO [73]. Thus, the absorption spectrum could also provide the fingerprints of graphene as the core of quantum dots [72]. To further explore the optical properties of GQDs, PL technique was also applied by using different excitation wavelength from 350 to 450 nm, and the results are shown in Fig. 4(a). Various excitation wavelengths were examined to monitor effect the subsequent excited energy state distribution due to confinement effect and their radiation recombination. The PL spectra revealed a Stokes shift and a rapid extinction with increasing the excitation wavelength [74]. The inset in Fig. 4(a) shows the GQDs aqueous solution exposed to a UV lamp radiation (355 nm).

Fig. 4(b) illustrates the UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra of the GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite samples with different fabrication methods as compared to the pure TiO2 porous. As shown in the figure, the TiO2 porous shows a strong absorption in UV region, and the absorption wavelength was approximately measured at 390 nm; which is well matched with the band gap energy of TiO2 in anatase phase [17]. By incorporating more amount of the GQDs to the pure TiO2 porous by using both spin coating and electrophoretic deposition methods, it was observed that the optical absorption edge is laid in visible region. The intensity of optical absorbance of the SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite is relatively higher than that of the EP-GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite in whole measured range of wavelengths. Concerning this and according to the SEM images and PL spectra, this observation can be ascribed to the more agglomerated distribution of GQDs on the surface of closely packed pores of the EP-GQDs/TiO2 sample.

PL technique was also used to investigate the radiative recombination rate of excitons and is presented in Fig. S4. We have found that the PL peak at 380 nm corresponds to the recombination between holes in the valence and electrons in the conduction band. The fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) is defined as the ratio of the number of emitted photons to the number of absorbed photons. For excitation at 320 nm, the FQY for the pure TiO2 porous, EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous and SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous were found to be 0.13%, 0.04% and 0.02%, respectively. These low FQY values correspond to the low recombination of electron-hole pairs which is useful in PEC reactions. Therefore, the addition of GQDs to TiO2 porous could improve the PEC activity due to retardation of electron−hole recombination.

Fig. 5 illustrates the photocurrent densities of the different samples as a function of the applied potential under dark and irradiation conditions. Based on the dark linear sweep voltammetry scan, all the photoanodes show an almost negligible photocurrent density under dark condition. Under irradiation, the SC-GQDs/TiO2 and EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposites as well as the pure TiO2 porous photoanodes exhibit the photocurrent densities of about 8, 3.8 and 2 A/m2, respectively, at 1 V potential versus Ag/AgCl. Both of the GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite photoanodes indicate higher photocurrent densities than the pure TiO2 porous photoanode, indicating that the GQDs play an important role in enhancing the PEC activity of TiO2 by increasing visible light absorption and charge carrier separation.

Furthermore, photoactivity of the synthesized photoelectrodes was also investigated by the chronoamperometry technique (transient photocurrent) at a constant potential of 0.5 V versus the Ag/AgCl reference electrode. Fig. 6 shows a good photosensitivity and stability for all of the prepared photoanodes. The photocurrent density of the samples increases by irradiation and decreases when the irradiation source is removed. In addition, the photocurrents of the photoelectrodes indicate a slight reduction in the first few seconds of each illumination time interval due to the charge carrier recombination process [75]. As shown in Fig. 6, the transient photocurrent density of the SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite, EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite and TiO2 porous photoanodes was about 6.35, 3.29 and 1.86 A/m2, respectively.

The kinetic of e−-h+ recombination was studied by measuring the photovoltage as a function of time during relaxation from the irradiated state to the dark state under an open circuit. Life time of the photo generated electrons in each energy state can be calculated by using the following relation [76]:
(1)
τ n
=
k B
T
e
d
V OC
/ d t
− 1
in here, k
B
T, t and e are thermal energy, time and elementary charge, respectively.

Fig. S5 shows the measured transient life times of all the samples in different energy states. It was found that the transient life time (τ) of the SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite photoanode is longer than those of the EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite and TiO2 porous photoanodes. The transient life time (τ) of the EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite photoanode was lower than the porous TiO2 photoanode. In the photocalytic system, literature indicates that the facile recombination between the photogenerated electrons and holes is the major factor that controls the photocatalytic efficiency [77]. Reddy et al. reported that the synergistic effect between carbon and TiO2 has the potential to tune recombination of photo-induced electrons and holes for photocatalysis [78,79]. In the spin coating method, GQDs were uniformly deposited on the TiO2 porous; as a result, the recombination rate of SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous was decreased as compared with the TiO2 porous and lead to an improved photoelectrochemical activity. Recently, a similar behavior has been reported for GQDs incorporated into TiO2 nanotubes by drop casting [51]. In contrast, the TiO2 porous surface was covered and saturated with GQDs in the electrophoretic method. Therefore, the GQDs act as recombination centers for charge carriers. It has been reported that a higher concentration of GQDs in TiO2 composites results in increased recombination rate and a lower improved of photocatalytic activity [80]. Thus, GQDs concentration can be influenced on photoelectrochemical activity of the composite.

Mott–Schottky (MS) plot (1/C 2 vs. V) describes the capacitance (C) of semiconductor depletion layer depending on the applied bias potential (V) and is given by equation below [81]:
(2)
1
C 2
=
2
e ε
ε 0
N D
A 2
V −
V fb
−
k B
T
e
where ε, ε 0, e, N D, A, V fb and k B T/e are the dielectric constant of the used semiconductor, the vacuum permittivity (8.85 × 10−12 C2/Nm2), the electron charge (1.602 × 10−19 C), the donor density, surface area of photoelectrode, the flat band potential and a temperature dependent correction term, respectively. The MS plot provides information on position of the flat-band potential (V fb) from the V-axis intercept and its slope has an inverse relationship with the donor density (N D) of semiconductor film. Moreover, the MS plot of the n-type and the p-type semiconductor possess positive and negative slope, respectively. MS plots for all the synthesized photoanodes are illustrated in Fig. 7 . As shown, the MS plots have positive slope, indicating n-type nature of all samples. The slopes of SC-GQDs/hierarchical porous TiO2 electrode and EP-GQDs/hierarchical porous TiO2 electrodes were smaller than TiO2 due to higher carrier density of GQDs. Recently, similar behavior was also reported for the CQDs/TaON [82]. Furthermore, the flat band potential (V fb) of the SC-GQDs/hierarchical porous TiO2, EP-GQDs/hierarchical porous TiO2 and hierarchical porous TiO2 electrodes were measured −0.97, −0.88 and −0.82 V, respectively, indicating the enhanced charge separation and transfer for GQDs/hierarchical porous TiO2 electrode with compared the TiO2 porous electrode. Whereas location of the V fb is situated just under the conduction band of n-type semiconductors, the conduction band edge of the hierarchical porous TiO2 can be determined by V fb. The conduction band (CB) energy level with Ag/AgCl as the reference can be calculated by using the following relation [83]:
(3)
E CB
eV
= − e
4 . 71 +
V fb
As a result, the E CB level of the TiO2 was obtained to be −3.89 eV. Finally, the valence band of the TiO2 porous was determined at about −7 eV.

To understand the role of GQDs in photoelectrochemical enhancement, we have considered current – voltage (CV) curve to study the GQDs energy state for determining their LUMO orbital as shown in Fig. S6. The peak observed at −0.12 V (in line (b)) is due to oxidation potential (E ox) of the GQDs. However, this peak was not found in the electrolyte (0.1 M KCl) without GQDs in line (a). The HOMO energy level relative to the vacuum level was determined at −4.28 eV by using the following relation [84]:
(4)
E
HOMO
= − e
E ox
+ 4.4
The GQDs possess a band gap energy about 2.2 eV obtained from UV–visible absorption spectrum of its solution (Fig. 4(a)). Thus, the LUMO energy level relative to the vacuum level is −2.08 eV.

Based on the above results, the mechanism for the photoelectrochemical water-splitting process by the SC-GQDs/TiO2 porous and EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposites could be proposed as schematically shown in Fig. 8 . In the SC-GQDs/TiO2 sample (Fig. 8(a)), the GQDs act as a photosensitizer like organic dyes to sensitize the TiO2 porous under visible light and donate electrons to the conduction band of TiO2. Eventually, the electrons are transferred to the Pt counter electrode for hydrogen production. While, in the EP-GQDs/TiO2 sample (Fig. 8 (b)), the high concentration of GQDs causes some pore blockage as shown in the SEM images (Fig. 2). Therefore, the photo generated electrons of the GQDs have a more chance to be recombined with holes and a less chance to reach the Pt counter electrode resulting in a lower photocurrent density.

In summary, we have explored the use of GQDs as a green sensitizer to build GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite as a photoelectrode for hydrogen production. The GQDs were deposited onto the pure TiO2 porous by both spin coating and electrophoretic deposition methods. Based on FE-SEM observations, the spin coated GQDs were uniformly anchored on the TiO2 porous while, the amount of GQDs deposited by electrodeposition was not well controlled and caused some pore blockage in the TiO2 porous. The UV–visible absorption spectra of the samples showed that the optical absorption of the GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite increased in visible region as compared with the pure TiO2 porous. The highest photocurrent density of 6.35 and 3.29 A/m2 was achieved for the SC-GQDs/TiO2 and EP-GQDs/TiO2 porous nanocomposite photoanode, respectively.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Iran National Science Foundation (Research Chair Award of Surface and Interface Physics) through a Grant Number 940009. The authors also would like to thank the Research and Technology Council of the Sharif University of Technology for partial support of the project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2017.05.301.
